era_context: The Eastern Roman Empire under Justinian I is in its final years, facing plague, military overextension, and fiscal crisis. The Sasanid Empire under Khosrow I is a major rival power. In China, the Northern and Southern dynasties continue their division. In Mesoamerica, the Classic Maya civilization is in its florescence.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Byzantine frontier (sparse documentation)
Oceania and Pacific islands (archaeological record only)

events.title: Nika Riots aftermath: continued reconstruction and religious tensions in Constantinople
events.description: In 562 CE, Constantinople was still recovering from the Nika Riots of 532 CE, which had devastated the city and killed tens of thousands. Justinian I had rebuilt much of the city, including the Hagia Sophia (completed 537 CE), but social and religious tensions remained. The riots had been sparked by tensions between the Blue and Green factions (chariot racing supporters) but had revealed deeper social and religious divisions. In 562, these tensions continued to simmer beneath the surface of Byzantine society.
key_figures: Theodora (empress, deceased 548 CE, but her legacy persisted)

sources.name: Procopius, History of the Wars and Secret History

events.certainty_note: The Nika Riots occurred in 532 CE; their aftermath and continued social tensions in 562 are inferred from broader historical context rather than specific documentation for this year.
cross_references: Part of broader social and religious tensions in Byzantine society

events.title: Justinian I continues reign in final years
events.description: In 562 CE, Justinian I, now in his late 70s, continued to govern the Byzantine Empire. His reign had lasted 35 years and was marked by ambitious military campaigns, legal reforms, and architectural achievements. In 562, Justinian was in declining health and would die in November 565. His final years saw continued military operations in Italy and the Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: Justinian's reign dates (527–565 CE) are well-established.

events.title: Plague of Justinian continues with recurring waves
events.description: The Plague of Justinian continued to recur in 562 CE, with documented outbreaks in Constantinople and other major cities. The plague had been ongoing since 541 CE and would continue in waves until the mid-8th century.
events.certainty_note: Multiple contemporary sources attest to plague recurrence in the 560s.
cross_references: Part of broader Plague of Justinian (541–750 CE)

events.title: Northern Zhou continues consolidation in northern China
events.description: The Northern Zhou dynasty continued its consolidation of power in northern China in 562 CE. Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou (r. 561–578 CE) was in the second year of his reign, overseeing military campaigns and administrative reforms.
key_figures: Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou (r. 561–578 CE)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Northern Zhou chronology is established, but specific events in 562 CE are not individually documented.
cross_references: Part of broader period of Chinese disunity (420–589 CE)

events.title: Chen dynasty maintains control of southern China
events.description: The Chen dynasty, ruling southern China from their capital at Jiankang (modern Nanjing), maintained control of the Yangtze River valley and southern territories in 562 CE. Emperor Wen of Chen (r. 559–582 CE) was ruling at this time.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Chen dynasty chronology is established, but specific events in 562 CE are not individually documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The narrative of the Nika Riots as a purely social or religious event has been revised by modern scholarship. Recent work emphasizes the riots' political dimensions and their role in consolidating Justinian's autocratic power. The riots are no longer viewed simply as a spontaneous uprising but as a complex event involving multiple social groups and political factions. The aftermath of the riots in 562 CE is not well-documented in surviving sources, and the extent of continued social tension is inferred rather than directly attested.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 562 CE is moderate for the Byzantine Empire, drawing on contemporary Greek sources. Chinese sources are abundant but compiled centuries later. The record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military history. Economic and social history of non-elite populations is underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas are absent from contemporary written sources.
